macro_segments.title: Shmitat Kesafim: The Obligation to Release and the Exception of Voluntary Repayment

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Shmitat Kesafim: The Creditor's Obligation to Say 'I Release You'
The Rosh's Interpretation: During Shmita the Debt Exists but Cannot Be Demanded; After Shmita It Is Automatically Annulled
Rabba's Position: Pressuring the Debtor to Say 'Nevertheless' and Voluntary Repayment
Abaye's Challenge: The Debt Must Not Be Framed as Debt Payment but as a Gift
The Story of Abba bar Marta: Applying Subtle Pressure to Encourage Voluntary Repayment

macro_segments.title: Pruzbul: The Mechanism to Circumvent Shmitat Kesafim and the Ease of Its Application

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's Ruling: A Person Can Claim He Had a Pruzbul and Lost It Without Producing It
The Rationale: People Would Not Abandon the Permitted Way of Exacting Debt
Rava's Application: Suggesting to the Debtor That He May Have Had a Pruzbul
Contradiction in the Mishnah: Creditor Must Produce Pruzbul vs. Sages Say Not Necessary
Resolution: Different Eras and Evolving Standards for Pruzbul Requirements

macro_segments.title: Enslaved Captive: Whether Redemption and Intent Determine Status and the Role of Ye'ush

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: A Slave Taken Captive—If Redeemed for Servitude He Returns; If Redeemed for Freedom He Goes Free
Rabban Gamliel's Dissent: Either Way He Remains a Slave
The Gemaric Question: What is the Case—Before or After Ye'ush?
Abaye's Answer: Before Ye'ush He Is Enslaved if Redeemed for Servitude; If Redeemed for Freedom He Goes Free as a Tikkun Olam to Incentivize Redemption
Rava's Answer: The Case Is After Ye'ush; The Sages Rule Based on Ye'ush and Shinui Reshut; Rabban Gamliel's Tikkun Olam Prevents Slaves from Running Away
Challenge from Baraita: Rabban Gamliel Says the Mitzvah to Redeem Slaves Equals That of Free Persons
Rava's Response: Rabban Gamliel Did Not Know What the Rabbis Were Saying; He Addresses Different Scenarios

macro_segments.title: Ownership of Slaves by Non-Jews: The Distinction Between Kinyan Guf and Ma'aseh Yadav

micro_segments.title: How the Captors Acquire Rights: Partial Ownership Through Ma'aseh Yadav, Not Full Property Ownership
Reish Lakish: Non-Jews Can Own Non-Jews Only for Ma'aseh Yadav; Jews Can Own Non-Jews as Full Property
Verse: 'From Them You May Buy Slaves'—Only Jews Can Own Non-Jews as Full Property
Kal va-Chomer: If Non-Jews Can Own Jews for Ma'aseh Yadav, Certainly They Can Own Non-Jews for Ma'aseh Yadav
Kibbush Milchamah: Captors Acquire Partial Rights Through Forceful Seizure in War
The Verse 'And They Took Captives': Establishes That Non-Jewish Captors Can Acquire Ownership of Captured Persons

macro_segments.title: Escape and Manumission: Freedom Through Flight and the Need for a Writ of Manumission

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: A Slave Who Escapes His Captors Goes Free and Receives a Writ of Manumission
Apparent Contradiction: Rabban Gamliel Says Always Enslaved, But Rabbi Yochanan Says Escape Brings Freedom
Abaye's Resolution: Rabban Gamliel Before Ye'ush, Rabbi Yochanan After Ye'ush
Rava's Position: Even After Ye'ush, the Tikkun Olam Sends Slaves Back to Masters, But Escape Is Different—The Slave Has Risked His Life

macro_segments.title: Ownership and Status: Ma'aseh Yadav Versus Kinyan Guf and the Role of Manumission Documents

micro_segments.title: The Case of Shmuel's Maidservant: Redeemed Before Ye'ush; After Ye'ush No One Wants Her; She Needs No Writ of Manumission
Shmuel's Position: One Who Renounces Ownership Goes Free Without a Writ of Manumission
The Verse: 'A Man's Slave'—Only When the Master Has Rights Does the Person Retain Slave Status
The Case of Rabbi Abba bar Zutra's Maidservant: Held by a Non-Jew; Freeing Her by Writ to Incentivize Her Redemption
Two Approaches to the Writ: Either to Incentivize Redemption or to Publicly Reveal Status and Lower Ransom Price

macro_segments.title: The Story of the Branded Woman: Public Proof of Slave Status and the Acceptance of Alternative Explanations

micro_segments.title: The Wedding in Lod: Guests Notice the Woman's Branded Arms
The Host's Explanation: She Was Tortured During Captivity for Refusing to Serve Idols
The Guests' Reaction: They Believed the Explanation and Honored Him More
The Gemara's Lesson: Credible Physical Evidence Allows for Alternative Explanations; Without Such Evidence, Claims Are Not Believed

topical_tags.term: Kinyan Guf (Ownership of Person as Property)
Rabba vs. Abaye (Debate on Pressuring Debtors)
Rabban Gamliel (Opposing View on Enslaved Captive)
Reish Lakish (Source for Non-Jewish Slave Ownership Rules)
Chizkiyah (Reason for Tikkun Olam to Return Captive Slaves)
